summary: Lynda Resnick discusses her approach to managing a portfolio of standalone companies by fostering an entrepreneurial spirit and allocating her time based on each company's life cycle. She emphasizes extreme attention to detail, the limitations of the interview process, and the ultimate importance of trusting one's instincts in hiring.

principles.statement: Allocate your time to the businesses that need you most based on their life cycle.
principles.rationale: Different companies require different levels of executive intervention depending on their maturity, current leadership strength, and marketing needs.
principles.source_quote: And so I go where I'm needed and I tend to have very big meetings these days... with a lot of people on board so they all get the message

principles.rationale: Structuring divisions as standalone companies and encouraging them to operate entrepreneurially is the key to their success.
principles.source_quote: each company runs in a very entrepreneurial way and one of the things that's rewarded at our companies is the entrepreneurial spirit. So they know that that's how you do well.

principles.statement: Do your due diligence, but ultimately trust your instinct in hiring.
principles.rationale: It is difficult to get the truth about a candidate's past performance, so after doing basic research, you must rely on your gut feeling.
principles.source_quote: you try to do your due diligence... And um you just go with your instinct, I'm afraid, in the end, you just do.

mental_models.name: Companies are like people
mental_models.description: Viewing businesses as having biological life cycles (e.g., toddler, mature) to determine how much executive intervention and reinvention they require.
mental_models.example: Palm was viewed as being in its "toddler stage," while Tela was considered "mature" and required Resnick to step in and reinvent it.

heuristics: You will know more about a new hire in their first three hours at work than in six months of interviews.
God is in the details; treat everything with the same importance.

signature_quotes.text: God is in the details. I'm It's awful. I can't help it. Everything has the same importance.
Companies are like people. They have a life cycle...
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why she spends more time with certain divisions over others.

signature_quotes.text: ...you can spend six months interviewing somebody and you'll know more the first three hours they're at work than you will ever know from those interviews.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the challenges of hiring and evaluating talent.

signature_quotes.text: ...you just go with your instinct, I'm afraid, in the end, you just do.
signature_quotes.context: On making the final call when hiring someone.

anti_patterns.description: Over-relying on the interview process to evaluate a candidate's fit and capabilities.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Interviews do not reveal how a person actually works; you learn more by observing them in their first three hours on the job.
